title: A Finance Commission Speech or a Speech of Slander?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The English title preserves the Tamil wordplay between `நிதி` (finance) and `நிந்தனை` (slander). The closing village saying, literally “Sugar, sugar, uncle; write it in the ledger and lick it,” mocks promises that exist only on paper. Source comparison confirms repairs including `மின் பற்றாக்குறைக்கு`, `அதைக்`, `வெளிநடப்பு`, `பி.எச்.ஈ.எல்.`, removal of the OCR fragment `ies`, `குறைக்கப்பட்டு`, `ஆளுங்கட்சியினர்`, and `2012ஆம் ஆண்டு டிசம்பரில்`. These changes are confined to the derived Tamil reading copy. The printed salutation is restored; canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: The Union government's Fourteenth Finance Commission met in Chennai on 16 December 2013. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa reportedly explained, without notes, the causes of Tamil Nadu's power shortage, complete with evidence. Perhaps because she had neither notes nor evidence in hand, she spoke as though everyone should accept several absurd claims as firmly established truths.

She spoke about electricity during the Yercaud by-election campaign too. She alleged that the Union government and the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam had conspired deliberately to reduce generation at Union government power stations in Tamil Nadu, thereby causing the state's shortage.

I explained in terms anyone could understand that production had fallen because maintenance was under way at Union power companies; that newspapers had already reported this on specified dates; that the DMK had no connection whatsoever with it; that neither the Union government nor a political party would conspire to reduce electricity generation in such matters; and that a person holding the office of Chief Minister should not make childish allegations. As usual, the Chief Minister then conveniently abandoned the issue.

But at the Fourteenth Finance Commission meeting on the 16th, she claimed that the Union government had withheld approval for Tamil Nadu's power-generation projects, deliberately denying it for political reasons, and granted approval only after the Supreme Court intervened. I expect the Union government to answer this accusation.

At the National Development Council meeting on 27 December 2012, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa hurried through her speech and staged a walkout. She said there that she had courageously decided to raise Tamil Nadu's electricity tariff. Should not someone celebrated for bravery and decisive action have directly asked there why the Union government had not approved these power projects? She could have asked that and then walked out. What use is there in shrinking from that good opportunity, returning here, and attacking the Union government as though brandishing a sword in empty air?

When a power shortage arose once under the party government, I immediately consulted the minister concerned, the Chief Secretary and department officials. I telephoned T.R. Baalu, then a Union minister, and had him meet the Prime Minister immediately. As a result of that meeting, the Prime Minister summoned the Union Power Minister, and the Union government agreed to supply 1,000 megawatts from 1 December 2008 until 30 April.
Jayalalithaa did not stop with saying the Union government withheld approval. As usual, she recited a litany of complaints against the DMK government:

> Between 2001 and 2006, I initiated many new power-generation projects. Had those projects been properly advanced and completed, Tamil Nadu could be a power-surplus state even today. But the previous government cast the new projects into the dust and completely discarded them. It showed no interest in advancing or completing them.

I have answered this charge many times. Yet she repeats it again and again like a trained parrot. When a person holding the office of Chief Minister makes an accusation and the other side replies, should she not read that reply and understand clearly what the truth is?

When the AIADMK came to power in June 2011, Jayalalithaa spent much of her reply to the Governor's Address discussing the electricity shortage. She said Tamil Nadu could have avoided the shortage had the DMK continued the efforts undertaken during her rule; that the existing three-hour cuts would be reduced to two hours from July; and that the party government had done nothing to increase generation. I gave a detailed answer in *Murasoli* on 18 June 2011.

Again, on 4 February 2012, while speaking for an hour and a half in reply to the Governor's Address, she said there would have been no shortage had the DMK implemented power projects during the preceding five years. I answered her in detail in *Murasoli* on 6 February. But whether she forgot my reply or imagined the people had forgotten it, she has now repeated this diversion before the Finance Commission.
Let me explain once more what was done for power generation after the DMK formed the government in 2006:

* A 600-megawatt project linked to the Ennore Thermal Power Station was started on 8 December 2006 at an estimated cost of 3,136 crore rupees. * A 600-megawatt project at Mettur Thermal Power Station, estimated at 3,100 crore rupees, was started on 2 May 2007. * A 600-megawatt project at North Chennai Thermal Power Station, estimated at 2,475 crore rupees, was started on 26 June 2007. * Two projects of 800 megawatts each were started at Udangudi on 15 October 2007 as a joint venture of BHEL and the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board, at an estimated cost of 8,362 crore rupees. * Another 600-megawatt project at North Chennai Thermal Power Station, estimated at 2,475 crore rupees, was started on 14 July 2008. * A 183-megawatt cogeneration project using cooperative and public-sector sugar mills, costing 1,126 crore rupees, was started on 14 December 2009.

Work on projects capable of generating a total of 4,183 megawatts began under the DMK government. One or two may have been conceived during the preceding AIADMK administration, but it was the DMK government that implemented them all. The projects above prove that the DMK never possessed the small-minded bitterness now shown by the AIADMK government, which says, “The other party planned it, so let us hold its funeral.”

Because of various measures taken after the DMK formed the government in 2006 with future demand in mind, provision was made for 1,400 megawatts in 2010-11, 3,316 megawatts in 2011-12, 1,222 megawatts in 2012-13 and 1,860 megawatts in 2013-14, a total of 7,798 megawatts through various projects.

I believe these figures provide the firm, evidence-based answer required for Jayalalithaa's accusation before the Finance Commission that no new generation projects were begun under the DMK government.

Speaking before the Commission, the Chief Minister said: “When I, Jayalalithaa, first served as Chief Minister from 1991 to 1996, Tamil Nadu was a power-surplus state. There were no power restrictions then.”

Was a system of power restriction ranging from twenty to thirty per cent in force in Tamil Nadu from 1 September 1991 during Jayalalithaa's 1991-96 government, or was it not? Even if the Chief Minister does not answer, will the department's officials do so?

Why, even earlier, was power restriction in force under an AIADMK government from 3 October 1982 to 26 July 1984, or was it not? Had the DMK government not established large numbers of factories after coming to power in 2006, no power restriction might have arisen. But can a government refuse to promote industrial growth for that reason?

The shortage under the DMK government arose because the AIADMK government had not initiated enough generating projects. Generation is now increasing at least to some extent because the DMK government began those projects. Instead of blaming the Union government and the party that previously governed the state, the present Chief Minister should properly encourage the projects planned under the DMK. Generation will then increase further.

Jayalalithaa said that “Tamil Nadu faced an electricity shortage throughout 2007,” meaning during DMK rule. To show how truthful that claim is, let me recall what Arcot N. Veerasamy, then Electricity Minister in the DMK government, said in the Assembly on 3 April 2007:
> We are a state with surplus electricity generation. Tamil Nadu is the only power-surplus state in India. We are now selling surplus electricity to Maharashtra. We are selling it to Punjab.

From this alone one can understand whether Tamil Nadu was power-deficient in 2007. Had there been a shortage under the DMK that year, could surplus electricity have been sold to Maharashtra and Punjab?

The Chief Minister also said yesterday: “Within the next six months, Tamil Nadu will become fully power-surplus and free from power cuts.” For Tamil Nadu's own good, it would be better if she stopped making such empty promises so often. Do you know why?
In June 2011, replying to the Governor's Address, she said: “From July, we will reduce Tamil Nadu's existing three-hour power cut to two hours.”
The financial statement read in the Assembly on 4 August 2011 said: “As generation increases, power cuts will be progressively relaxed and completely eliminated throughout the state by August 2012.”

Do you know the headline in her official newspaper on 5 February 2012 for her reply to the Governor's Address the previous day? “From June 2012, the power shortage will be progressively reduced and will be completely eliminated by the middle of 2013.” Any ruling-party member who doubts this may find the old newspaper and see.

In a February 2012 interview with *India Today*, Tamil Nadu's Electricity Minister said: “Tamil Nadu will experience a power shortage only from this March to May. If we endure these three months, there will be no shortage in Tamil Nadu.”
In December of that same year, they announced in the Assembly: “From the coming June, the duration of power cuts will be progressively reduced.”

After giving such assurances repeatedly, they have now announced that Tamil Nadu will become fully power-surplus and free of cuts within the next six months. When earlier promises repeatedly went unfulfilled, will anyone believe the latest AIADMK assurance?
Do you know what else they said in their 2011 election manifesto?

> Darkened Tamil Nadu will be made radiant. Swift action will be taken to supply uninterrupted electricity for homes, industry and agriculture. A special scheme will be implemented as soon as the AIADMK assumes office. By 2012, within two years, 1,000 megawatts will be generated from municipal waste in 151 municipalities and corporations. By 2013, ten 300-megawatt “solar energy parks” will be created, generating 3,000 megawatts. Steps will be taken to produce 200 kilowatts of biomass power in 160 selected village panchayats. This will create 64,000 new rural jobs.

The people of Tamil Nadu read this manifesto, believed it true, voted, and brought them to power. Have they fulfilled even one of these promises? Voters of Tamil Nadu, please think for one minute! The AIADMK makes promises to obtain votes, not to fulfil them.

“Sugar, sugar, uncle; write it in the ledger and lick it!” says a village expression. Reading the AIADMK manifesto and “Amma's speeches” brings that saying to mind. It also raises the question: was Jayalalithaa's speech a Finance Commission speech, or a speech of slander?
With affection,
M.K.
